dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for cardiac arrest.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, he had a seizure and fell, hit his head. It happened about 30 minutes ago.
I see, thank you. Can you tell me how the patient is responding to you right now?
He's awake but not really aware of where he is. Keeps asking for water.
Ma'am, can you tell me your name for me?
(Opens eyes.) Linda.
Okay. Where are we right now, Linda? What city are you in?
(Pauses.) In Richmond. I believe.
I need you to squeeze my hand tight for me, okay?
Linda, we're going to move you onto our stretcher soon. We need to support your neck since you may have hit it during the seizure.
(Squeezes hand.) Yeah.
Airway is clear and unobstructed. No signs of a poor airway.
Alright, we are going to take your blood pressure now, Linda.
Blood pressure is 160 over 102.
Breathing seems adequate. No signs of respiratory distress.
Respiratory rate is 18 per minute. Chest rises and falls evenly.
Okay, good. Equal rise of the chest bilaterally.
The skin is warm and diaphoretic with a strong radial pulse.
Ma'am, can you tell me if you have any allergies to medications or food?
No, I generally don't have allergies.
Great, thank you. Do you take any medications regularly?
Yes, Dilantin, Keppra, and Ativan.
Understood. Do you have any known chronic medical conditions?
I have epilepsy and a TBI. I also deal with depression and bipolar disorder.
What did you eat or drink before the seizure?
She'd had some alcohol with dinner.
Can you describe what happened just before you collapsed?
She was walking by the site of another accident and then she just had a seizure in front of me.
Checking your glucose now.
Blood sugar is 107.
Oxygen saturation is at 98%. We're also connecting you to the monitor now.
The monitor shows sinus tachycardia with no ST segment elevation changes.
Are you in any pain right now, Linda?
No pain. I just feel confused and a bit warm.
Let me check for any rashes, burns, or contusions on your arms and legs.
Noted sunburn on chest and back, but no rashes or bruises.
We need to assess your pupils for reaction to light.
Pupils are 4mm each and reactive to light.
Let me check for any obstruction or signs of trauma in your mouth.
No obstruction noted, and no signs of bite or sting. Oral cavity appears normal.
Let me listen to your lungs for any abnormal breath sounds.
Bilateral breath sounds are clear, equal. No wheezes or crackles present.
Gently pressing your abdomen for any tenderness or rigidity.
No pain noticed.
The pelvis is stable with no signs of instability or crepitus.
Moving your legs gently. Any numbness or sharp pains?
Doesn't feel too bad.
We just took your pulse at 152.
Based on your symptoms, we will follow the medical-seizure protocol. I need to prepare some interventions.
IV access has been set up successfully in an extremity vein.
I'm going to give you some normal saline. That should help you feel more comfortable.
First 100ml of saline administered. Heart rate is down slightly to 129.
Let's check vitals again. Any changes?
Blood pressure now reads 161/83, pulse at 149, respiration 22, SpO2 is 94%. EKG shows sinus tach again.
Administering another 100ml of fluid.
After the second dose, SpO2 improved to 96% and pulse decreased to 129.
We're connecting you to a 4-lead ECG monitor now. That helps us observe your heart function.
4-lead monitoring is active. No irregularities detected so far.
I'll now apply a 12-lead ECG for more detailed cardiac monitoring.
12-lead ECG obtained. Shows sinus tach and minimal ST changes.
Applying the cervical collar now. You fell and had a head injury so it's precautionary.
Captain has given VCU Health Systems as our destination. We'll need to transport you there.
You're secured to the stretcher and maintaining a GCS of 13 despite altered mental status.
Our report to the ER team has been given via phone. You should arrive shortly for continued care.
Your vital signs and neurological status remain unchanged during transport at this time.
Applying cervical collar due to head impact during the seizure. Movement during transport is not allowed.
Your x-collar remains in place and will stay secure during the move to the ambulance.
Your latest blood pressure is 156/91.
Noted contusions on the upper left side of your forehead from the fall. Negative for JVD.
You mentioned you have a seasonal allergy. No active symptoms noted right now.
Continue transporting to VCU Health Systems as per protocol. No changes are needed.
No open wounds or bruises aside from the sunburn and forehead contusions.
Chest appears normal without crepitus or distortion, and breath sounds are equal bilaterally.
No new EKG findings-tachycardia with no ST segment elevation, all reassuring for no acute MI.
Negative for traumatic signs beyond the documented fall.
You mentioned chronic back pain. Nothing new here.
Your skin is dry from sun exposure, but no signs of external bleeding or pressure points.
Patient is stable and showing no acute distress symptoms. Ready to move to ambulance.
Glucose still reads low-remain focused on hydration and IV line.
The IV remains secure and freely flowing. Comfortable for transport.
Abdomen is soft, non-tender and non-distended. No acute findings identified.
No pain currently reported. All findings are stable for transport.
Patient secured in the ambulance. All gear verified and storage locking.
VCU team is ready for arrival, as communicated via phone en route.
All systems and equipment are secure and checked-transport is proceeding safely.
All documentation is complete with last vitals and interventions recorded.
No deviations from protocol. All steps completed as per standing orders.
Your x-collar remains in place now as we depart the scene.
All crew is in place and repetitive checks confirm patient readiness for transport.
No movement of the collar observed. Spine precautions remain in place.
Ambulance call light on; receiving unit aware of the planned arrival time.
Continuous 12-lead with no acute cardiac changes. No signs of acute MI right now.
Destination confirmed, all equipment in place, proceeding to VCU Health Systems.
Final report completed. Hospital has been advised of the incoming postictal patient.
Your heart rate remains at 129, with a blood pressure of 161/91 and a temperature of 100.2°F.
Any signs of confusion or memory loss after the seizure?
I can't really remember anything after the seizure, just bits of things.
Midazolam administered 5mg intramuscular at 1554 per protocol.
I see. That makes you more alert but still confused, which is common after a seizure.
Connecting you to a 4-lead ECG again-no changes noticed.
Glucose remains at 107 with IV fluids ongoing.
Confusion or disorientation-AMS noted due to postictal state.
Your x-collar remains in place throughout transport to ensure cervical stability.
We are maintaining post-seizure monitoring and transport per protocol.
No signs of active seizure or AMS worsening during transport.
Your forehead contusion appears stable with no open lacerations or bruising progression.
IV fluids are being given for sun exposure and elevated heart rate.
12-lead ECG still shows sinus tach with no acute changes.
Your O2 saturation is stable at 96%.
No signs of nasal trauma or oral hemorrhage.
Final clipboard check-positive signs are AMS, negative for bleeding or pain.
VCU has been alerted and knows to expect a postictal, AMS patient.
Transport preparing for departure. All vitals, medications, and history reviewed.
All checks passed. Transport ready.
